claims.claim_text: Argo Workflows is a container-native workflow engine for orchestrating parallel jobs on Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: line 28 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The official Argo Workflows overview states this directly.

claims.claim_text: Tekton is a cloud-native CI/CD solution focused on pipelines.
claims.span_hint: line 28 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: Tekton's official getting-started page describes Tekton as an open-source cloud native CI/CD solution.

claims.claim_text: Argo Workflows is designed for DAG- and step-based workflows and is used for graph-shaped workloads such as data processing, machine learning, and CI/CD on Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: line 28 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: The official overview explicitly mentions DAG and step-based workflows plus CI/CD, data processing, and machine learning use cases.

claims.claim_text: Large fan-out workflows can overwhelm Kubernetes control-plane capacity if pod creation is left unbounded.
claims.span_hint: line 30 | section: Why This Module Matters
claims.rationale: Argo's scale guidance warns that large amounts of work and parallel nodes can overwhelm the Kubernetes API and require explicit controls.

claims.claim_text: The Workflow Controller reconciles Workflow objects and generated workflow pods, and each Step or DAG Task causes a pod to be created.
claims.rationale: The architecture documentation describes controller reconciliation and states that each Step and each DAG Task generate a pod.

claims.claim_text: Production installs should use a specific Argo Workflows release version instead of the moving `latest` target.
claims.span_hint: lines 107-109 | section: Installing Argo Workflows Safely
claims.rationale: The installation guide says `latest` is the tip of `main` and recommends using a specific release version in production.

claims.claim_text: In Argo, `steps` model sequential stages with optional parallel steps, while `dag` models tasks by dependency and runs tasks without dependencies immediately.
claims.span_hint: lines 194-202, 254-255 | section: From a Single Container to a Graph
claims.rationale: The core concepts page describes both `steps` and `dag` with these execution semantics.

claims.claim_text: A `WorkflowTemplate` is a workflow definition that lives in the cluster and can be referenced by other Workflows and WorkflowTemplates.
claims.rationale: The WorkflowTemplate documentation defines it this way and explains cross-reference behavior.

claims.claim_text: Workflows that use artifacts need a configured artifact repository, and Argo supports S3-compatible backends such as AWS, GCS, and MinIO.
claims.rationale: Argo's artifact repository docs say artifact workflows must configure a repository and list supported backends including AWS, GCS, and MinIO.

claims.claim_text: `withItems` iterates over a static list, while `withParam` iterates over a JSON array that can be produced at runtime.
claims.span_hint: lines 551-552, 591-591 | section: Loops, Fan-Out, and Controlled Parallelism
claims.rationale: The loops documentation defines `withItems` and `withParam` exactly in these terms.

claims.claim_text: Argo `parallelism` can restrict the total concurrent executions of steps or tasks within a workflow or template.
claims.rationale: The synchronization docs state that `parallelism` restricts concurrent task or step executions within a workflow or template.

claims.claim_text: Argo provides native `retryStrategy`, `continueOn`, and `activeDeadlineSeconds` fields for retries, optional-failure continuation, and per-template timeouts.
claims.span_hint: lines 831-908 | section: Error Handling, Timeouts, and Debugging
claims.rationale: The Argo field reference defines all three fields and their semantics.

claims.claim_text: Argo Events Sensors can create Workflows or submit WorkflowTemplates from webhook events and parameterize workflow fields from event payloads.
claims.span_hint: lines 950-999 | section: Argo Events and External Triggers
claims.rationale: The Argo Events workflow-trigger documentation shows webhook-triggered workflow creation, WorkflowTemplate submission, and parameterization from event data.

claims.claim_text: The war-story setup presents an unattributed incident as if it were a specific historical event.
claims.anchor_text: A financial analytics team once used Argo Workflows to backtest many strategy variants. The first prototype worked beautifully with a few dozen inputs, so the team scaled the input list without changing the workflow-level controls. The workflow did exactly what it was told to do: it tried to create an enormous number of pods quickly.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A large fan-out workflow can overload a cluster if the input set grows without parallelism caps, batching, and other control-plane safeguards.
claims.rationale: This is a specific incident-style anecdote with no attribution, so it should be rewritten as a general operational lesson.

claims.claim_text: The Airflow comparison sentence makes an unsupported popularity claim about where Airflow is commonly used.
claims.span_hint: line 1428 | section: Choosing Argo Workflows, Tekton, or Airflow
claims.anchor_text: Airflow remains common for scheduled data workflows with rich operator ecosystems and external-system orchestration.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Airflow can be a good fit when the workload is mostly scheduled coordination of external systems rather than Kubernetes-native pod execution.
claims.rationale: The "remains common" claim is not directly supportable from an allowlisted primary source and should be rewritten as general guidance.

claims.claim_text: The Airflow table row makes a load-bearing vendor-fit claim that is better expressed as general tool-shape guidance than as an uncited factual assertion.
claims.span_hint: line 1434 | section: Choosing Argo Workflows, Tekton, or Airflow
claims.anchor_text: | Nightly warehouse orchestration across many SaaS APIs | Airflow | Mature scheduling and provider ecosystem can reduce custom glue. | Use Argo if each stage is containerized and Kubernetes is the execution plane. |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Nightly warehouse orchestration across many SaaS APIs | Airflow or another scheduler-oriented workflow tool | If the work is mostly scheduled coordination of external systems, a scheduler-oriented tool can reduce custom glue. | Use Argo if each stage is containerized and Kubernetes is the execution plane. |
claims.rationale: This row currently asserts a specific Airflow fit without an allowlisted primary source that directly backs the wording.

claims.claim_text: The Argo project includes Workflows, CD, Events, and Rollouts.
claims.rationale: The Argo project homepage lists these four tools directly.

further_reading.title: Argo Workflows Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary overview for Argo Workflows positioning, DAG support, and core Kubernetes-native use cases.

further_reading.title: Running At Massive Scale
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly relevant to the module's guidance on bounded parallelism, API pressure, and safe large-workflow design.

further_reading.title: Argo Events: Argo Workflow Trigger
further_reading.why_relevant: Shows how Sensors create Workflows and parameterize them from incoming event payloads.

further_reading.title: Tekton Getting Started
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful counterpart for the module's Argo-versus-Tekton comparisons.

notes: No shared citation pool exists yet for the ci-cd-pipelines section, so supported claims use direct proposed URLs. Code blocks, quiz answers, and hands-on exercise steps were excluded; the Airflow comparison prose and table row were flagged because their current wording makes uncited vendor-fit claims that are better rewritten as general guidance.
